coredata.dc:title: Non-doped and non-modified carbon dots with high quantum yield for the chemosensing of uric acid and living cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots without heteroatoms-doping and surface modifications were designed to be a novel chemosensing strategy on the quantitative detection of uric acid (UA) with the aid of uricase-induced enzymatic reaction and Fenton reaction. In this work, ascorbic acid (AA)-derived carbon dots (A-CDs) were prepared in the mixture of ethanol and water via one-step hydrothermal synthesis at a relatively low temperature (120 °C) for 10 h. The resultant A-CDs were proved to be excitation-independent. When excited at the wavelength of 420 nm, the nanodots displayed green fluorescence (535 nm) which was then linearly quenched as UA concentration increased in the range of 0–56 μM, according to which the detection limit was calculated to be 0.49 μM. With regards to the excellent sensitivity and selectivity to UA, real sample assay was performed on the A-CDs detection system, which provided relatively reliable recoveries of UA contained in human serum/urine. Besides, in view of the high quantum yield, the A-CDs were applied to live-cell imaging assay and were considered to become an alternative tracer tool in biomedical imaging.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are co-first authors of this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots without heteroatoms-doping and surface modifications were designed to be a novel chemosensing strategy on the quantitative detection of uric acid (UA) with the aid of uricase-induced enzymatic reaction and Fenton reaction. In this work, ascorbic acid (AA)-derived carbon dots (A-CDs) were prepared in the mixture of ethanol and water via one-step hydrothermal synthesis at a relatively low temperature (120 °C) for 10 h. The resultant A-CDs were proved to be excitation-independent. When excited at the wavelength of 420 nm, the nanodots displayed green fluorescence (535 nm) which was then linearly quenched as UA concentration increased in the range of 0–56 μM, according to which the detection limit was calculated to be 0.49 μM. With regards to the excellent sensitivity and selectivity to UA, real sample assay was performed on the A-CDs detection system, which provided relatively reliable recoveries of UA contained in human serum/urine. Besides, in view of the high quantum yield, the A-CDs were applied to live-cell imaging assay and were considered to become an alternative tracer tool in biomedical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decades, a large number of novel molecular markers have been discovered with the advent of advanced research tools, providing a new horizon for the metabolic mechanisms of diseases [1–3]. Inside the human body there exists a complex metabolic network which is regulated by small biomolecules and aids to complete necessary interactions among numerous pathways. As the ultimate product of purines, uric acid (UA) is one of the major antioxidants and is mostly excreted from kidneys during metabolism. Under normal conditions [4], serum uric acid is in the range of 2.6–5.73 mg/dl (that is 154.8–341.1 μM) with a mean of 4.58 mg/dl (that is 272.6 μM) in healthy adults, while the urine uric acid levels ranges from 2.7 to 5.4 mg/dl (that is 160.6–321.2 μM) with a mean of 3.68 mg/dl (that is 219.04 μM). Once the dynamic balance of UA is broken by too much purine intake, excess production of endogeneous purine, increased purine metabolism and renal dysfunction can arise. According to recent studies, elevated UA has relevance to cardiovascular diseases caused by the abnormal precipitation and deposition of UA [5]. Besides, a low level of UA can affect the smooth functioning of antioxidant processes in the body, which may involve infertility due to oxidative stress [6]. On these points, UA serves as a biomarker not to be overlooked for certain malignant conditions, thus monitoring UA in biological matrices is of great importance in clinical diagnosis and treatment.

Typically, the linear change of light absorption at a specific wavelength of ∼470 nm which is the characteristic absorption peak of UA is adopted for UA detection in hospital. Other than this, HPLC-UV [6,7], chromatography [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [10] and electrochemical-surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (E-SERS) [11] are prevailed alternative approaches for the lab or clinical use. Meanwhile, in order to accelerate the turnover time and obtain better selectivity and sensitivity, diverse electrochemical methods [12–14] have been developed relying on innovative electrodes or that modified by functional particles. Lately, Ana P. Lima et al. [15] demonstrated the development of 3D-printed graphene/polylactic acid electrode for the amperometric determination of UA in biological fluids with a dynamic range of 0.5–250 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.2 μM. Tayebeh Iranmanesh et al. [16] synthesized CeO2 nanoparticles-decorated CNTs to modify glassy carbon electrode to form a modified electrode, which was used for the simultaneous determination of UA and some other biomolecules. Instead of using rigid electrodes, Rinky Sha et al. [17] proposed a new way of fabricating a flexible electrochemical sensor for non-enzymatic detection of UA and the sensor exhibited a LOD of 1.169 μM with UA ranging from 10 μM to 400 μM. Although great progress has been made, among the current electrochemical methods, dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA) and acetaminophen (AC) are often chosen as concurrent analytes because the three substances share similar redox peaks on the cyclic voltammetry figure, which brings challenges to electrode preparation. By contrast, carbon dots (CDs), a new zero-dimension carbon-based material has been springing up in scientific research [18,19] and has offered numbers of exciting possibilities in various research fields [20–25] since 2004 [26]. Using CDs as fluorescent probes is able to provide nanometer-scale precise determination of specific analytes, even in sophisticated environment such like human fluids containing unexpected interference factors. Unlike metal-based quantum dots [27], CDs have low biotoxicity and comparable luminescence performance [28]. However, it is a common case that most of the CDs reported were doped heteroatoms in order to enhance fluorescence intensity [29–32]. Among the reported CDs on UA detection, catalytic metals such as Fe, Cu and Zn [33,34], or non-metals such as N, P and S [35–37] that can provide more active sites for reaction are often selected as doping elements to enhance the decomposition rate of hydrogen peroxide, thus increasing the sensitivity of UA. Nevertheless, these doped CDs often require demanding synthesis conditions and many of them have short emission wavelength in blue light area [36,38,39], which to some extent limits their biological application.

Herein, to broaden the biological application and meanwhile simplify the synthesis procedure of fluorescent probes, we used an antioxidant substance, ascorbic acid, as a single raw material and successfully synthesized non-doped CDs (A-CDs) emitting bight green fluorescence with high quantum yield. The A-CDs possess a better biological tissue penetrability than blue-glowing CDs and thus lead to a great potential for cellular imaging. In this work, ascorbic acid was chosen as the carbon precursor and was dissolved in the mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water at a volume ratio of 1 to 1. Instead of a high-temperature calcination, a relatively moderate temperature of 120 °C was set for reaction. After 10-h adequate dehydration and carbonization, clear yellowish brown solution with charcoal flavor was preliminarily prepared. Following the subsequent dialysis, evaporation, lyophilization and redissolution, the final A-CDs aqueous solution showed fascinating luminescence properties and manifested excellent quenching phenomenon in the presence of UA with the aid of the uricase-induced enzymatic reaction as well as the catalyzation triggered by low-dose Fe2+ ions, which suggested a good performance of the A-CDs in UA detection and determination. Furthermore, the prepared A-CDs were proved to perform cellular imaging by distributing mainly on the cell membrane and sparsely in the cytoplasm. Considering the capability of living cell imaging and the marvelous response to hydrogen peroxide, this A-CDs aqueous system is expected to become an alternative approach in the future to both hydrogen peroxide detection and in vivo imaging of reactive oxygen species (ROS).

The reagents and chemicals used in this work were l(+)-Ascorbic acid (AA, C6H8O6, 99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), Ethanol (ETH, C2H6O,≥99.8%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), uirc acid (UA, C5H4N4O3, 99%, Alfa, England), uricase (250UN, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 1X, pH 7.2–7.4, Adamas, Shanghai, China), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥30%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, 98%, Alfa, England), rhodamine 6G (R6G, C28H31N2O3Cl, 95%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), urea (CH4N2O, ≥30%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), allantoin (C4H6N4O3, 98%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.5%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), anhydrous glucose (Glu, C6H12O6, 96%, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), l-cysteine (Cys, C3H7NO2S, 99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥96%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5%, Greagent, Shanghai, China), Glutathione (GSH, Solarbio, Beijing, China), Lactose (98%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD, 99%+, Adamas, Shanghai, China), Cholesterol (CHOL, 99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), l-Proline (99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), l-Alanine (99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), Glycine (99%, Adamas, Shanghai, China), Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA), fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, USA), penicillin-streptomycin (5000 U/mL, Gibco, USA), trypsin-EDTA (phenol red, 0.25%, Gibco, USA) and WST-1 (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl]−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide, 5 mg/mL, Beyotime, China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water except for uricase and the A-CDs for cell imaging, which needed to be preliminary diluted with PBS solution. All agents were of analytical grade and used as received without previous treatment.

Simple one-step hydrothermal method was chosen to develop green fluorescent A-CDs exhibiting excitation-independent emission spectra using AA as the sole precursor. In our typical preparation, 0.3 g AA powder was dispersed in the mixed solvent of ETH and ultrapure water with an ultimate volume of 20 mL, among which the volume ratio of ETH to ultrapure water is 1 to 1. After 5 min of ultrasound, a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was used to hold the clarified mixture to be heated at 120 °C for 10 h in a laboratory baking oven. After being cooled down to room temperature, the resultant solution with clear light brown color was ready to be transferred into a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, which needed undergoing overnight dialysis through a 1000-MWCO dialysis bag against ultrapure water. Finally, the A-CDs within the bag was collected into a round-bottom flask and concentrated by rotary evaporation and lyophilization for further analysis.

A transmission electron microscopy (H-600) was chosen to obtain the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. A Tecnai G2 20 microscope (FEI, USA) at 200 kV was used to capture the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image. The data on particle size distribution were measured by a Zetasizer nano instrument (Malvern, England). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were taken on a ProStar LC240 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA).

FLThe ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were acquired through a U–3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) when the slit width and scan speed were equal to 2 nm and 1200 nm/min, respectively. A F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Tech Co. LTD, Shanghai) were used to perform all the fluorescent tests, where we set the slit width to 5 nm and the output gain to middle-range level. In order to better study or exploit the luminescence process, it is necessary to set the appropriate operating parameters when using a U–3900H spectrophotometer, including scan wavelength, slit width and scan speed. Slits that are too wide can lead to a reduction in resolution, potentially masking the original absorption peaks, while slits that are too narrow can result in weak fluorescence signal not suitable for effective detection. As for scan speed, speed that is too fast can cause higher noise and lower resolution that may lead to a jagged peak shape, while speed that is too low may extend testing time and affect the quality of the sample. Therefore, different combinations of slit width and scanning speed have been tried in this work. The results show that the lowest baseline noise and ideally smooth absorption curves can be obtained with the slit width and scan speed being set to 2 nm and 1200 nm/min, respectively. In addition, some of the fluorescence spectra presented herein were treated with numerical normalization, through which each of the fluorescence (FL) intensity values were divided by their maximum intensity value, turning the data into dimensionless probability distribution between 0 and 1 for a better comparison.

As for the evaluation of QY of the A-CDs, regarding that the excitation and emission wavelengths of the A-CDs at 420 nm and 535 nm are close to those of R6G, R6G with QY of 0.92 in water [40] was identified as the standard reference in our work. Typically, A-CDs and R6G were diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water whose refractive index η is 1.33. Afterwards, the absorbance and fluorescence of both A-CDs and R6G were measured while being excited at a wavelength of 420 nm. The absorption value of each solution was controlled below 0.05 to minimize the interference of self-absorption. Finally, the QY of the A-CDs was calculated referring to the following Eq. [41],
Q C

where ‘Q’ stands for the QY value; ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent; ‘I’ signifies the FL intensity; ‘A’ denoted the absorption value. The subscript letters ‘C’ and ‘R’ represent A-CDs and R6G, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the A-CDs on UA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Optimizing the experimental conditions. First of all, in order to achieve maximum QY, we kept the temperature unchanged and optimized the synthesis conditions of the A-CDs by regulating the amount of AA, the heating time, as well as the solvent ratio of ETH to ultrapure water. Secondly, in order to achieve excellent detection performance, some important parameters, including reaction temperature, reaction time and the amount of the A-CDs and uricase, were then optimized by fluorescence method through the F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer. In brief, various volumes (10, 50, 100, 200, 250, 400, 500, 1000 μL) of UA aqueous solution (340 μM) were incubated with a certain enzymatic activity of uricase (10 μL, 4 U/mL) at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, the as-prepared A-CDs (100 μL, 6 mg/mL) and Fe2+ ions (6 μL, 0.01 M) were added in turn to the mixture. After homogenization and incubation for 30 min at 40 °C, the mixed solution was diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water and was subsequently transferred into a quartz cuvette to measure the corresponding fluorescence spectrum excited by 420 nm laser.

Fluorescence assay of UA. The operating procedure of the fluorescent detection on UA is the same as those under the optimized conditions mentioned above. Besides, in order to assess the stability of the A-CDs-Fe(II), fluorescent intensity changes of the A-CDs-Fe(II) dispersed in solutions with different temperature, irradiation time and pH values were recorded, respectively. Furthermore, to appraise the selectivity of the detection system to UA, a few common interfering substances were chosen in coexistence with UA to investigate their impact on the FL intensity of the A-CDs-Fe(II).

To demonstrate the practicality of A-CDs-Fe(II) probe, we collected two drug-free human serum and urine samples from Suzhou Science and Technology Town Hospital. Before the experiment started, the samples underwent a pretreatment step which is necessary to remove the interferences as a result of suspended impurities and certain protein molecules in serum and urine. The serum samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min while the urine samples were kept under 10,000 rpm for 10 min. After the pretreatment, the centrifuged supernatant of all the samples were extracted and diluted 10 fold and 50 fold with ultrapure water respectively. As for the final analysis, 100 μL of each diluted supernatant and each of the various spiked samples prepared by the standard addition of UA (Serum sample: 1.89 and 7.56 μM; Urine sample: 2.26 and 5.29 μM) were separately added into the detection system composed of uricase, A-CDs and Fe2+ ions. All experiments and measurements were repeated three times (n = 3) with the testing procedure as described in section 2.5.

Human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cells were cultured with high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere to ∼80% confluency. Following the culture process, cell suspension needed to be prepared and the cells in the suspension solution were quantified by using a hemocytometer. For each study, two precautions must be taken when preparing samples with lower concentration of the A-CDs: (a) Fully mix the A-CDs stock solution by pipetting it several times before using a micropipette to draw up low-dose A-CDs solution. (b) For each concentration, prepare in advance the mixed solution with complete DMEM cultures in a labelled centrifugal tube and then add equal amount to each replicate.

WST-1 assay was chosen to assess the cytotoxicity of the A-CDs. Cells (5 × 103 cells in 100 μL per well) were prepared by being diluted in complete DMEM and were then seeded in a 96-well plate at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated with 100 μL complete DMEM containing different concentrations of the A-CDs (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2 mg/mL, six replicates for each concentration) for another 24 h. The culture media was then discarded and the cells were carefully washed 2 times with PBS, followed by the addition of 100 μL fresh DMEM and 10 μL WST-1 for a 2-h incubation at 37 °C. The absorbance of the resultant Formazan was monitored at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Synergy HT, Bio Tek). Three blank replicates containing only DMEM and WST-1 was set up to reduce background interference. Data were normalized using the absorbance of untreated cells as controls (twelve replicates). The cell viability can be expressed as the equation below,

For imaging assay, cells (5 × 103 cells in 100 μL per well) were prepared by being diluted in complete DMEM and were then seeded in a 96-well plate at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere for 12 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated with 100 μL complete DMEM containing different concentrations of the A-CDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1 mg/mL, three replicates for each concentration) and were placed at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Then, the supernatant was discarded, after which the cells were carefully washed three times with PBS solution to remove the residual A-CDs. Finally, 100 μL PBS solution was added into each well to stabilize cellular state. The cellular images under bright field and blue excitation light (465 nm) were captured on an inverted phase contrast fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ti2–U, Nikon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of materials and synthesis conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To realize fast and specific quantification of UA, we used uricase to first break down UA into H2O2 and allantoin to indirectly detect UA by detecting H2O2, which shifted our demand to the development of H2O2-sensitive CDs. AA, or vitamin C, is a typical antioxidant small molecule and polyol structurally similar to glucose. As reported in current studies [42,43], the enediol group contained in AA is unstable and can be easily oxidized to diketone group to become dehydroascorbic acid, which can be further hydrolyzed in alkaline solution or strongly acidic solution and thus lose its activity. Moreover, not only that the AA molecule has its own conjugated double bond as the prerequisite for the formation of luminescent structures, but its small molecular weight is able to facilitate the dehydration and carbonization during CDs preparation. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the precursor material of the A-CDs was determined as AA, whose molecular structure contains enediol and lactone bonds that are both easily oxidized. In order to improve the efficiency of the reaction, we added ETH to the solvent with the intention of establishing the esterification between the hydroxyl group on ETH and the carboxyl group of AA-deprived products (Scheme S1). Meanwhile, in the presence of an acidic environment caused by the ionization of H+ from AA, two molecules of aldehyde or ketone can interact with each other and induce the classical aldol reaction among the products, which in the following results proved to generate an increased degree of conjugation like CC bonds and a red-shifted emission spectrum. Under a relatively lower temperature of 120 °C, we optimized the synthesis conditions of the A-CDs from the amount of AA, the volume ratio of ETH:H2O and the reaction time through the comparison of QY. Up until that point, 3-dimensional spectra of the CDs using 0.3 g AA, 120 °C, and 10-h heating at different volume ratios of ETH:H2O were analyzed and presented in Fig. 1 . It was evident that as the volume ratio of ETH:H2O increased, the emission spectrum were first red-shifted and then blue-shifted, performing green emission in line with expectations only when ETH:H2O equaled 1:1 or 3:1. The emergence of red-shift was largely influenced by the proceeding degree of the acetal reaction between ETH and AA, which further affected particle size and degree of conjugation. Though increasing proportion of ETH led to a larger particle size and a higher degree of conjugation, excessive ETH (ETH:H2O > 3:1) was not favorable to the dissolution of AA in the solvent. This reduced the amount of AA involved in the acetal reaction and thus weakened the green light emission signal. As a result of optimization, the as-prepared CDs using 0.3 g AA, the volume ratio of ETH:H2O at 3:1 and the reaction time for 10 h were proved to generate the highest QY (Fig. S1). However, the highest QY may not give rise to the best testing results. As verified by Fig. S2, it can be notably observed that with all the other variables being consistent, the linearity obtained from the CDs with the highest QY was not good enough. Instead, the detection was more effective by using the A-CDs to construct the UA detection probe. We speculated that the difference between the two CDs occurred because the solvent controlled the degree of dehydration and carbonization of the precursor. During the one-step hydrothermal reaction, the AA molecules together with the solvent successively underwent dehydration, polymerization and carbonization to form nanometer-scale CDs. High proportion of ETH could achieve a fuller acetal reaction in this work, but at the same time made carbonization aggravate, which resulted in a decreased amount of the surface functional groups sensitive to H2O2. From the inset photos in Fig. S2, the A-CDs were obviously more likely to aggregate and precipitate than the CDs dispersed in the solvent with higher proportion of ETH, because the rigidity of the A-CDs was weakened to a large extent by the abundant flexible branched chains on the surface of the A-CDs, which tended to agglomerate the nano particles and thus could form precipitation more easily. Based on the above results, the A-CDs were adopted for the subsequent detection experiments.

To confirm the synthesis of the A-CDs, both physicochemical and photoluminescent methods were employed to verify the morphology, composition optical properties of the A-CDs, respectively. TEM image was acquired to characterize the morphology of the A-CDs at nanometer scale. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) showed that the particles of the A-CDs were uniformly distributed in the solution and nearly spherical in shape. Through the Zetasizer nano instrument, we measured the size distribution of the A-CDs. The nanoparticle size of the A-CDs varied between 3.25 and 5.05 nm and their average diameter was 4.25 nm according to the size distribution histogram (Fig. S3) counted by around 100 particles. Then, to further identify the graphitic structure of each particle, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) was captured under high-resolution TEM so that the crystal lattice spacing (0.19 nm) that has similarity with the (104) lattice fringe of certain graphitic carbons [44] could be clearly visible.

Next, the measured FT-IR spectrum was presented in Fig. 2c to identify the surface functional groups and bonding composition of the A-CDs. By the analysis of the transmittance peaks, abundant hydrophilic groups including hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and carbonyl (CO) groups emerged on the surface of the A-CDs, not only enhancing the water solubility but also providing adequate oxidizable reaction sites. As shown by the high frequency region in the spectrum, the characteristic peaks at 3351, 2982 and 2904 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of alcoholic or phenolic hydroxyl groups could be clearly seen. In addition, the peak at 1727 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of carboxyl CO, while the appearance of 1630 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of carbonyl CO or double-bond CC. By contrast, the low frequency region (fingerprint region) below 1300 cm−1 contained more complicated information, but the peaks of the stretching vibration of the ester bond (C–O–C) were still visible at 1280 and 1208 cm−1, which may result from the esterification between ETH and AA or the residual structure caused by the incomplete reaction of AA during the pre-preparation of the A-CDs. Meanwhile, some C–H bending vibrations of substituted benzene were produced at 1203, 870, 820 and 760 cm−1, and the out-of-plane bending vibration of –OH belonging to carboxylic acid was also found at 929 cm−1.

Subsequently, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured to characterize the optical properties of the aqueous solution of the A-CDs. As observed in Fig. 3 a, the A-CDs solution apparently peaked at ∼250 nm (black line), which is relevant to the electron transition from π to π∗ of aromatic sp2 domain (CC) and the electron transition from n to π∗ of aromatic heterocycle (CO) [45]. Besides, there existed a broad transition band between 250 nm and 500 nm, which can be attributed to the surface trapping states as a major source of fluorescence emission [46]. In Fig. 3b, by setting a 10-nm increment to the excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured. The emission spectra indicated that the A-CDs were excitation-independent in the green emission area, which largely resulted from the relatively homogeneous particle sizes [47] and surface states [48]. Thereafter, through the given equation above, the resultant QY of the A-CDs was calculated to be as high as 35% at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm.

Experiments at different temperature, irradiation time and pH values were conducted to evaluate the stability of the A-CDs in variable conditions. Fig. S4 displayed that the A-CDs system exhibited a good temperature stability (Fig. S4a) and a good FL durability within a 2-h irradiation (Fig. S4b). This also indicated that the A-CDs could withstand light exposure for quite a long time, which posed a positive effect on the long observation likely to occur in the living cell imaging assay. Besides, as pH value increased, there was no obvious variation in the solution with pH values between 5 and 10 showing in the FL intensity of the A-CDs, only sharp variation appearing in strong acid and alkali environment whereby the quenching effect of Fe2+ acting on the A-CDs was weakened by overly alkali solution but boosted by acidic solution containing excessive H+ (Fig. S4c). The results reflected that the A-CDs was capable of handling detection work in a complicate environment.

While controlling UA at 2 μM, the enzymatic reaction time at 1 h, the concentration of Fe2+ at 20 μM and the reaction time of A-CDs-Fe(II) system at 30 min, we optimized the active unit of uricase (Fig. S5a) and the temperature of enzymatic reaction (Fig. S5b), and tested the quenching efficiency at different pH values from 3 to 7 (Fig. S5c). The original pH value of the A-CDs-Fe(II)-Uricase system was around 6. Next, 1% hydrochloric acid (HCl) and 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were used for titration assay. It is noteworthy that a moderate amount of HCl enhanced the fluorescence of the system. On the contrary, when pH value was greater than 7, the fluorescence of the system was too faint to perform UA detection. Therefore, pH values between 3 and 7 were chosen to further determine the effect of pH on analytes during measurement. As presented by the line graph of Fig. S5c, the quenching efficiency decreased with the increasing pH values, but a rise appeared at pH equal to 6, where there was no addition of HCl or NaOH. According to the results, the acidic environment was more favorable to the occurrence of Fenton reaction, for Fe(II) ions was less likely to form complex when co-existed with H+. However, due to the obvious promotion of the fluorescence of the A-CDs-Fe(II) system caused by H+ ions at pH = 4 and pH = 5, the quenching phenomenon at pH = 4 and pH = 5 was less pronounced than that at pH = 3, thus leading to the non-unidirectional trend of the quenching efficiency as pH value increased from 3 to 6.

In addition, we compared the catalytic effect of different metal ions and demonstrated that the catalytic effect of Fe2+ ions in this experiment was much higher than that of the other two metal ions (Cu2+ and Mn2+) commonly used to catalyze H2O2 (Fig. S5d). Afterwards, kinetic study was monitored to decide the final concentration of the A-CDs in solution and the appropriate reaction time of the detection system (Fig. S6). During the exploration of the final concentration of the A-CDs, it was noteworthy that less A-CDs could lead to a shorter detection time but with the corresponding linearity being not ideal after many times of verification, while higher dosage of the A-CDs was extremely possible to induce an unobservable quenching phenomenon owing to the thick covering of the fluorescence variation caused by the strong fluorescence of the A-CDs themselves. In summary, the following experimental conditions using A-CDs-Fe(II) as detection probe were found to contribute both the best linearity and the lowest LOD: (a) Active unit of uricase: 0.06 U; (b) Incubation time of enzymatic reaction: 60 min; (c) Temperature of detection: 40 °C; (d) Final concentration of the A-CDs: 0.2 g/L; (e) Response time of quenching experiment: 35 min. As a result, the total detection time observed for the proposed A-CDs-Fe(II) system in the experimental setup is 95 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UA detection assay using the A-CDs-Fe(II) system
ce:section.ce:para: Before the formal assay, 15 μL of 4 U/mL uricase loaded in a series of centrifuge tubes (5 mL) was first incubated with different concentrations of aqueous UA solution (from 0 to 113 μM) in a water bath at 40 °C for 1 h. Then, 100 μL of the A-CDs solution (6 mg/mL) and 6 μL of the as-prepared ferrous chloride solution (0.01 M) were added in turn to the solution, after which the volume was fixed to 3 mL by ultrapure water, where the concentration of the A-CDs was about 0.2 g/L and the concentration of Fe2+ ions was 20 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, after the reaction at 40 °C for 30 min, the FL intensity spectra were measured as UA concentration increased and were indicative of a quenching intensity as UA concentration increased. Moreover, the FL intensity was almost completely quenched when the concentration of UA reached 113 μM. Fig. 4b revealed that the FL quenching intensity of the A-CDs at 535 nm by UA was found to be linearly dependent on dose of UA concentration from 0 to 56 μM. The linear regression equation is FL = (254.43 ± 1.65) + (−2.63 ± 0.06) [UA], μmol L−1 and the regression coefficient R2 was equal to 0.997. The LOD was calculated to be 0.49 μM using the criterion 3ε/S (ε = 0.37, S = 1356.69) [49], which should be low compared with those from the current articles (Table S1).

Additionally, in terms of the chemical properties of the given functional groups in Fig. 2c, the response mechanism for UA detection by the A-CDs-Fe(II) system can be basically sketched. First, UA was specifically oxidized and decomposed by uricase to produce allantoin and H2O2. In our work, UA and one of its oxidation products, allantoin, do not quench carbon dots on their own, but the other oxidation product, H2O2, can achieve fluorescence quenching by the aid of Fenton reaction which rapidly broke H2O2 down into free radical intermediates (•OH and •OOH) [36] that oxidized Fe(II) ions to Fe(III) ions and meanwhile destroyed the surface state and the fluorescence center [50] of the green-emission A-CDs.

The quenching mechanism of the free radicals to the A-CDs is obvious. As reported in previous studies [51], compounds with the structure of quinones are known as electron acceptors which can induce fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots. Since the precursor, AA, contains a high proportion of –OH group and two adjacent enol forms, the surface of the resultant A-CDs carries a large number of these functional structures that are easily attacked by free radicals, producing quinone-like substances that cause apparent diminishing changes in the FL intensity of the A-CDs.

In addition, we founded that Fe(III) ions could quench the A-CDs better than the same concentration of Fe(II) ions. Therefore, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Fe(III) ion, another substance during the decomposition of H2O2, was also investigated. Data on fluorescence lifetime and absorption spectra have been added into the revised manuscript and supplementary material. As shown in Fig. S7a, the A-CDs showed no new absorption peak after the addition of Fe(III), which means that there were no static intermediate formations in the quenching process. We further tested the change of fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S7b). According to the previous article [52], the short fluorescence lifetime of these A-CDs is indicative of the radiative recombination of the excitons giving rise to fluorescence. As the concentration of UA increases, up to 20 μM of Fe(III) ions may be produced under ideal circumstances. After the addition of 20 μM Fe(III), the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 2.23 ns to 1.92 ns, which indicates the existence of dynamic quenching. It has been previously reported that Fe(III) ions as quenchers can quench fluorescence as soon as they are added [50], but according to Fig. S7c in our work, the fluorescence variation after the addition of 20 μM Fe(III) followed by an incubation of 5 min was obviously less than that after the addition of 56 μM UA presented in our UA standard curve (Fig. 4).

Based on the discussion above, we have drawn a conclusion that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the A-CDs occurred during UA detection process was composed of both surface state change by free radicals and dynamic quenching by Fe(III) ions, but was dominated by free radicals overall.

For the repeatability, three-times experiments were conducted to make sure that all the experiments are repeatable. As to the reproducibility, we synthesized three bottles of A-CDs in the same way as mentioned in our method to investigate the consistency of the quenching results of the three corresponding systems. As results (Fig. S8) showed, though the LODs may be different from one another, the detection systems based on all three A-CDs were capable to quench UA in the range 0–56 μM with a good linearity, which demonstrated the reproducibility of the proposed sensor in our work.

In this part, various potential interfering substances that exist in human fluids, including ascorbic acid (AA), allantoin (Alla), urea (Urea), glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), lactose, l-Cysteine, β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), cholesterol (CHOL), l-Alanine, l-Proline, Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Cl− were chosen to assess the anti-interference of our UA detection probe. The concentration of UA was kept at 50 μM while the interfering substances were set to 500 μM. The normalized FL intensity in Fig. 5 a illustrated that the interfering substances caused no significant effect on the FL intensity of the probe system itself. Besides, no obvious interference was observed on the detection quality of UA (50 μM), even though UA coexisted with different chosen substances. Nevertheless, GSH, lactose, l-Cysteine, β-CD and AA caused visible interference on the detection of UA (Fig. 5b), which may result from the presence of groups prone to consume free radicals. The groups can be the –SH groups from GSH and l-Cysteine, as well as the –OH groups from lactose and β-CD. Overall, it can be confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of this probe system for UA detection was selective and hardly interrupted by ions or biomolecules except for some carbohydrates rich in –OH groups and some substances with strong reducing properties.

To further investigate the capability of our detection system in real samples, we applied it to the UA detection of two human serum and urine samples obtained from the local hospital. As listed in Table 1 , the recoveries for UA measurement were in the range of 82.64–91.87% and with the RSDs in the range of 1.7–4.6% (less than 5%). To test the accuracy of the proposed method, ADVIA 2400 Chemistry System (SIEMENS, Germany) was used to determine the UA concentration. Fig. S9 shows the results obtained from the developed sensor and clinical results of ADVIA 2400 Chemistry System. The relative error (%) of our method is 8.7 and 14.6 for the two clinical samples of 308 μM and 225 μM, respectively. Briefly speaking, the A-CDs-Fe(II) probe has the potential to determinate UA in human fluids, but the accuracy needs further improvement and optimization compared with the instrument used in clinic.

To investigate whether the A-CDs are potential for biological applications or not, cytotoxicity of the A-CDs was first evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, over 94% cell viability can be reached after the HeLa cells incubating with the A-CDs at 1 mg/mL for 24 h, indicating a good biocompatibility of the A-CDs. Furthermore, HeLa cells were incubated with various concentrations of the A-CDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8 and 1 mg/mL) at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere to perform cell imaging. Fluorescence microscopy was used to visualize the cellular uptake of the A-CDs after 24 h incubation. When the cells treated with 0.1 mg/mL A-CDs, there was no fluorescence signal being observed. When the cells treated with over 0.2 mg/mL A-CDs, crystallized A-CDs emerged with a poor cell morphology (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 6 , observed fluorescent signals using 0.2 mg/mL A-CDs were localized on the cell membrane and in the cytoplasm, especially strong interactions with the cell membrane (Fig. 6e and f). The nonuniform distribution of fluorescence in the cytoplasm may be attributed to A-CDs aggregation within certain cellular organelles (e.g., endosome or lysosome). In addition, green fluorescence was still observed and cell morphology was well maintained after 24 h incubation (Fig. 6d). In a nutshell, the results of cell imaging assay demonstrated that the A-CDs possessed a low cytotoxicity at 0.2 mg/mL to HeLa cells and preferred to attach to the cell membrane, which means the A-CDs can be potentially applied to cell membrane imaging of living cells.

The performance parameters of A-CDs-Fe(II) such as linear range and measured LOD in the proposed work were compared with the various other state-of-the-art designs available in the open sources. As presented in Table S1, the LODs obtained using fluorescence detection are often lower than those of other methods such as electrochemistry (EC) and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) technique. Besides, it can be observed that the materials used in the methods except for fluorescence method require complex fabrication process, including coating, arching, activation reaction and so forth that are time-consuming. In contrast, the materials in our work are easy and inexpensive to obtain, and the operations of preparing the sensor are quite simple with one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Among the listed methods using fluorescence method, our proposed sensor also achieved improvement in the sensitivity with a comparable low LOD. Moreover, the excellent biocompatibility of the proposed A-CDs-Fe(II) is quite promising and attractive for bioimaging applications.

In this work, we synthesized excitation-independent A-CDs emitting bright green fluorescence via simple one-step hydrothermal conversion by using a single raw material, AA, at 120 °C for 10 h. Combined with both uricase-induced enzymatic reaction and Fenton reaction catalyzed by Fe2+ ions, such A-CDs were able to be the effective probes for UA detection in aqueous solution as well as in real samples. The solution first underwent an enzymatic reaction, followed by a rapid decomposition of enzymatic H2O2 catalyzed by Fe2+ ions. Fenton reaction led to the formation of free radicals which quenched the fluorescent centers, namely the functional groups, on the surface of the A-CDs. This strategy displayed a low LOD (0.49 μM), a relatively broad linear range (0–56 μM) and a perfect ability of anti-interference. Though the experimental results proved that the A-CDs enabled to be a feasible tool in UA determination, the recoveries of the real samples assay here still need improving. Importantly, thanks to the high QY and the low cytotoxicity, the A-CDs gave a desirable result in HeLa cells imaging assay. Overall, the A-CDs in this paper were believed to be a selective and sensitive UA sensing probe and were suitable for bioimaging applications.
